startScreen.startScreenSubtitle: A 45-year-old woman presents with progressive muscle weakness. Work through this case applying your knowledge of skeletal, cardiac, and smooth muscle histology and the sarcomere.

endScreens.endScreenSubtitle: You correctly identified histological features of all three muscle types and demonstrated understanding of sarcomere structure.
You showed some understanding but confused features between muscle types. Review nuclear position, striations, and intercalated discs.
Revisit the distinguishing histological features of skeletal, cardiac, and smooth muscle, plus sarcomere structure.

params.text:
Case Presentation

A 45-year-old woman presents with progressive proximal muscle weakness over 3 months. A muscle biopsy is taken from the quadriceps for histological examination.

Under the microscope, the biopsy shows long, cylindrical fibres with visible cross-striations. The nuclei are multiple and located at the periphery beneath the sarcolemma — confirming this is skeletal muscle.

The basic contractile unit of striated muscle is the sarcomere, bounded by Z lines on either side.

branchingQuestion.question: The pathologist also examines a cardiac tissue sample from a post-mortem case. Which histological features distinguish cardiac muscle from skeletal muscle?

alternatives.text: Central nuclei, visible cross-striations, and intercalated discs at cell junctions
Peripheral multiple nuclei with no intercalated discs

feedback.title: Not quite
feedback.subtitle: Peripheral multiple nuclei describe skeletal muscle. Cardiac muscle has centrally placed nuclei and unique intercalated discs at cell-to-cell junctions.

alternatives.text: Spindle-shaped cells with a single central nucleus and no striations

feedback.title: Incorrect
feedback.subtitle: You described smooth muscle. Cardiac muscle has centrally placed nuclei, visible cross-striations, AND intercalated discs — the key distinguishing feature.

params.text:
Further Investigation

Correct! Cardiac muscle has central nuclei, cross-striations, and intercalated discs.

The patient also has gastrointestinal symptoms. A biopsy of the intestinal wall shows the muscularis layer. The smooth muscle cells here are spindle (fusiform) shaped with a single central elongated nucleus and no striations.

The pathologist notes that unlike striated muscle, smooth muscle lacks organised sarcomeres but still contracts using actin-myosin interactions regulated by calcium-calmodulin rather than troponin.

branchingQuestion.question: Returning to the skeletal muscle biopsy, the pathologist identifies sarcomeres. What are the boundaries of each sarcomere?

alternatives.text: Z lines (Z discs) on either side define each sarcomere
M lines on either side define each sarcomere

feedback.title: Not quite
feedback.subtitle: The M line is the midpoint of the sarcomere (middle of the A band). The boundaries are the Z lines (Z discs) on either side.

alternatives.text: Intercalated discs on either side define each sarcomere

feedback.title: Incorrect
feedback.subtitle: Intercalated discs are unique to cardiac muscle at cell-cell junctions. The sarcomere is bounded by Z lines (Z discs) on either side.

params.text:
Clinical Synthesis

Excellent! Summary of muscle histology:

  • Skeletal muscle: Multiple peripheral nuclei, cross-striations, sarcomeres bounded by Z lines
  • Cardiac muscle: Central nuclei, cross-striations, intercalated discs
  • Smooth muscle: Spindle-shaped, single central nucleus, no striations
  • Sarcomere: Basic contractile unit, bounded by Z lines (Z discs)

These histological distinctions are essential for identifying tissue types in biopsies and understanding muscle pathology.
